Antimicrobial Resistance Overview

Mechanisms of Resistance AMR Global Report (PDF)

Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is one of the greatest public health threats facing humanity today. When microorganisms such as bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites evolve mechanisms to withstand the drugs designed to kill them, infections become harder to treat and the risk of disease spread, severe illness, and death increases significantly. AMR occurs naturally over time through genetic mutations, but the misuse and overuse of antimicrobials in humans, animals, and agriculture is accelerating the process at an alarming rate.

What are antimicrobials?

Antimicrobials are agents used to prevent, control, and treat infectious diseases in humans, animals, and plants. They include antibiotics, antivirals, antifungals, and antiparasitics. Antibiotics are the most commonly known type of antimicrobial, used specifically to treat bacterial infections. The discovery of penicillin by Alexander Fleming in 1928 revolutionised medicine and has saved countless millions of lives. However, the effectiveness of these life-saving medicines is now under serious threat due to the emergence and spread of drug-resistant organisms across the globe.

How does resistance develop?

Bacteria and other microorganisms can develop resistance to antimicrobial agents through several mechanisms. These include enzymatic degradation of the drug, modification of the drug target site, active efflux of the drug from the cell, and reduced permeability of the cell membrane. Horizontal gene transfer allows resistance genes to spread between different species of bacteria, accelerating the dissemination of resistance traits through microbial communities. Selective pressure from antimicrobial use drives the survival advantage of resistant strains, leading to their predominance in clinical and environmental settings.

Global burden of AMR

The global burden of antimicrobial resistance is staggering. According to a landmark study published in The Lancet in 2022, an estimated 4.95 million deaths were associated with bacterial AMR in 2019, with 1.27 million deaths directly attributable to drug-resistant infections. These figures surpass the death tolls from HIV/AIDS and malaria combined, establishing AMR as one of the leading causes of death worldwide. Low- and middle-income countries bear a disproportionate burden due to limited access to clean water, sanitation, healthcare infrastructure, and second-line antibiotics.

One Health approach

Addressing AMR requires a One Health approach that recognises the interconnection between human health, animal health, and the environment. Approximately 60 percent of emerging infectious diseases originate in animals, and antimicrobial use in livestock and aquaculture contributes significantly to the development of resistant organisms that can transfer to humans through the food chain, direct contact, or environmental pathways. Coordinated surveillance, stewardship, and infection prevention and control programmes across all sectors are essential for containing the spread of drug-resistant infections and preserving the efficacy of existing antimicrobials for future generations to benefit from modern medicine.

Economic impact

The World Bank has estimated that AMR could cause global GDP losses of up to 3.4 trillion USD per year by 2030 if left unchecked. The economic impact extends beyond direct healthcare costs to include losses in productivity, trade, livestock production, and tourism. Without effective antimicrobials, routine medical procedures such as caesarean sections, joint replacements, organ transplants, and cancer chemotherapy would become significantly more dangerous. The development pipeline for new antibiotics remains insufficient, with few novel classes of antibiotics discovered in recent decades and limited commercial incentives for pharmaceutical companies to invest in antimicrobial research and development.

Key actions needed

Combating AMR requires coordinated global action across multiple fronts. National action plans aligned with the WHO Global Action Plan on AMR should be implemented by all countries. Antimicrobial stewardship programmes must be strengthened in healthcare facilities to ensure appropriate prescribing practices. Investment in research and development of new antibiotics, diagnostics, and alternative therapies is critical. Surveillance systems must be expanded to monitor the emergence and spread of resistant organisms. Public awareness campaigns should educate communities about the responsible use of antimicrobials and the importance of infection prevention measures including hand hygiene, vaccination, and safe food handling practices to reduce the burden of antimicrobial resistance worldwide.
